caveats: a: The occupation mix is for all telecommunications, not 517121, and includes large facilities-based carriers with extensive field work.
a: BLS provides occupation headcount and wages rather than wage-weighted task exposure.
a: Consumer MVNO, prepaid calling, wired resale, and enterprise telecommunications resellers have different labor models.
a: The injected compensation ratio is an ancestor-code proxy for NAICS 517 and likely reflects network-owner capital and wholesale economics unlike resellers.
rho: No source directly measures five-year AI implementation in 517121.
rho: Automation readiness differs between API-native MVNOs and firms dependent on old carrier, billing, or channel systems.
rho: Errors involving identity, billing, emergency access, service suspension, or regulatory reporting carry disproportionate cost.
e: The code definition establishes recurring resale activity but not lower-middle-market eligibility or transferability.
e: Carrier dependence and change-of-control provisions can make an otherwise recurring customer book nontransferable.
e: The frozen dataset has no defensible n-band value, so firm availability remains mechanically missing regardless of this estimated share.
s5: No source measures the five-year control-transfer rate for lower-middle-market telecommunications resellers.
s5: High-profile MVNO acquisitions demonstrate feasibility but are not representative transaction probabilities.
s5: Subscriber migration, carrier consent, licenses, numbers, and customer contracts may transfer differently from corporate control.
q: No cited source measures retention of implemented AI benefits in 517121.
q: Wholesale network cost, not labor, may dominate total economics even though q applies only to implemented gross benefit.
q: Consumer prepaid, cable MVNO, affinity, and enterprise contract pricing have different pass-through dynamics.
d5: BLS output covers facilities-based carriers, satellite, and other telecommunications as well as resellers.
d5: The FCC evidence is strongest for mobile and cable MVNOs rather than wired and prepaid calling resellers.
d5: Growing total traffic does not guarantee growth in reseller customer relationships or constant-quality service quantity.
o: No source directly measures the future operator-required share for 517121.
o: Network owners can vertically integrate successful reseller segments or alter wholesale terms.
o: A branded accountable operator may persist even when activation, billing, and support are largely automated.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers are households, small and midsize businesses, enterprises, affinity groups, and specialized demographic or usage segments. Revenue is typically recurring subscription, usage, line, seat, or bundled service charges, with economics driven by the spread between customer revenue and wholesale network, interconnection, device, software, support, regulatory, and acquisition costs.
lens.excluded_activities: Facilities-based wired and wireless carriers, satellite telecommunications and satellite resellers, wireless sales agents classified in 517122, equipment-only dealers, captive group purchasing, one-off brokers, shells, and firms without a recurring external-customer service relationship.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market mobile virtual network operators, prepaid calling-service resellers, and wired or wireless telecommunications resellers that purchase network access or capacity and repeatedly sell branded telecommunications services to unaffiliated business or household customers without operating the underlying transmission facilities.

a.bridge: BLS reports employment for broad NAICS 517000 Telecommunications, not a wage-weighted task mix for 517121. The broad sector includes 29.67% installation, maintenance, and repair work tied largely to facilities that Census says resellers do not operate, while it also includes 17.82% sales, 16.04% computer and mathematical, 14.85% office support, 8.05% business operations, and 7.33% management. I remove most network construction and field-repair weight and map reseller customer support, order entry, activation, billing, provisioning, fraud review, sales research, proposal drafting, account monitoring, churn outreach, knowledge retrieval, reporting, and routine technical triage as exposed; negotiation, enterprise solution design, escalations, security, compliance accountability, vendor management, and complex network diagnosis remain human. Broad telecom data likely understate reseller exposure because facilities work is overrepresented, but may overstate it if small resellers outsource support or rely heavily on high-touch enterprise sales.
caveats: The occupation mix is for all telecommunications, not 517121, and includes large facilities-based carriers with extensive field work.
BLS provides occupation headcount and wages rather than wage-weighted task exposure.
Consumer MVNO, prepaid calling, wired resale, and enterprise telecommunications resellers have different labor models.
The injected compensation ratio is an ancestor-code proxy for NAICS 517 and likely reflects network-owner capital and wholesale economics unlike resellers.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task survey of 517121 firms separating customer care, activation and provisioning, billing, fraud, sales, channel management, network operations, compliance, and executive work across consumer and business resellers would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The reseller model concentrates labor in digital commercial and service workflows rather than physical network operation. O*NET shows structured customer-service and service-sales tasks that combine records, orders, questions, proposals, and account maintenance with interpersonal selling, problem resolution, and negotiation.

d5.bridge: BLS projects constant-dollar output for all NAICS 517000 Telecommunications from $674.3 billion in 2024 to $876.8 billion in 2034, a 2.7% annual rate, not five-year demand for reseller service quantity. Roughly five years of that rate anchors near 1.14. I set a nearby base and widen for declining legacy voice and calling-card demand, growing data consumption, cable and other MVNO gains, eSIM and digital distribution, bundling, and possible carrier consolidation. Broad output may overstate reseller demand if network owners capture growth directly, or understate it if reseller share expands through differentiated brands and bundles.
caveats: BLS output covers facilities-based carriers, satellite, and other telecommunications as well as resellers.
The FCC evidence is strongest for mobile and cable MVNOs rather than wired and prepaid calling resellers.
Growing total traffic does not guarantee growth in reseller customer relationships or constant-quality service quantity.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year service-line forecast using reseller subscribers, lines, usage, churn, average constant-price revenue, wholesale capacity, MVNO market share, enterprise seats, and legacy voice volumes would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Connectivity demand should continue growing in volume and importance, and the FCC describes increasingly relevant cable MVNO competition and many MVNO providers. Reseller demand is less certain than total telecommunications because it depends on access to wholesale economics, differentiation, customer acquisition, and carrier channel strategy.

caveats: The code definition establishes recurring resale activity but not lower-middle-market eligibility or transferability.
Carrier dependence and change-of-control provisions can make an otherwise recurring customer book nontransferable.
The frozen dataset has no defensible n-band value, so firm availability remains mechanically missing regardless of this estimated share.
e.change_evidence: A matched FCC Form 499, Numbering Resource Utilization, commercial-company, wholesale-contract, customer, and transaction database classifying firms by ownership, revenue, normalized EBITDA, subscriber mix, churn, carrier concentration, contract assignability, and regulatory standing would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: Recurring external-customer subscriptions are intrinsic to the reseller definition, so many firms fit the service lens. Eligibility is reduced by firms outside the EBITDA band, carrier-controlled brands, thin or nontransferable wholesale agreements, customer concentration, prepaid businesses with weak retention, commoditized books without proprietary distribution, misclassified agents, regulatory deficiencies, and entities whose value depends on a single founder, carrier, or affinity partner.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 517 ($71,420,936,579) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 517 ($668,853,509,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 517. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.3470 (margins.json[517]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Telecom. Services EBITDA/Sales 34.70%); revenue/employee $711,427 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 517); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: No source directly measures the future operator-required share for 517121.
Network owners can vertically integrate successful reseller segments or alter wholesale terms.
A branded accountable operator may persist even when activation, billing, and support are largely automated.
o.change_evidence: Carrier channel strategy, direct-versus-reseller subscriber cohorts, eSIM activation data, embedded-connectivity contracts, customer support and complaint ownership, wholesale agreement terms, MVNO closures, and software-only service penetration would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Carrier direct sales, embedded connectivity, self-service eSIM activation, app-based support, and software-managed communications can eliminate some reseller quantity or compress the operator into a thin brand layer. A reseller remains accountable for plan design, customer acquisition, contracting, billing, identity, fraud, provisioning, service support, complaints, regulatory filings, vendor coordination, and continuity across the wholesale network, especially for specialized segments and enterprise customers.

caveats: No cited source measures retention of implemented AI benefits in 517121.
Wholesale network cost, not labor, may dominate total economics even though q applies only to implemented gross benefit.
Consumer prepaid, cable MVNO, affinity, and enterprise contract pricing have different pass-through dynamics.
q.change_evidence: Plan- and cohort-level data showing price, wholesale unit cost, renewals, churn, support cost, acquisition spend, credits, fraud, channel commissions, contribution margin, and realized savings before and after automation would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Monthly plans and existing enterprise contracts can preserve implemented labor savings until renewal, while low marginal administrative cost can reward scale. Retention is eroded by intense price competition, aggressive promotions and bundles, customer churn, carrier wholesale repricing, minimum commitments, channel commissions, device subsidies, fraud, service credits, regulatory cost, and the expectation that digital self-service should lower prices or improve support.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year AI implementation in 517121.
Automation readiness differs between API-native MVNOs and firms dependent on old carrier, billing, or channel systems.
Errors involving identity, billing, emergency access, service suspension, or regulatory reporting carry disproportionate cost.
rho.change_evidence: Controlled deployments at consumer and business resellers measuring contact deflection, handle time, provisioning errors, fraud losses, churn, sales conversion, regulatory complaints, escalation rates, integration cost, and fully loaded recurring AI expense would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Resellers already operate through digital billing, CRM, provisioning, ticketing, fraud, and carrier interfaces, making knowledge retrieval, interaction summaries, agent assistance, self-service, sales drafting, anomaly triage, and routine retention workflows implementable within five years. Constraints include carrier integration, legacy billing, identity and fraud risk, outage escalation, privacy, emergency-service obligations, consumer-protection rules, hallucination risk, vendor lock-in, and the need for accountable human handling of disputed charges and service failures.

caveats: No source measures the five-year control-transfer rate for lower-middle-market telecommunications resellers.
High-profile MVNO acquisitions demonstrate feasibility but are not representative transaction probabilities.
Subscriber migration, carrier consent, licenses, numbers, and customer contracts may transfer differently from corporate control.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year cohort linking FCC transfer and Form 499 records, carrier approvals, private M&A databases, subscriber-book sales, failed transactions, and the eligible 517121 firm stock would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Subscriber books, brands, billing systems, distribution relationships, and carrier contracts can support control acquisitions, and the FCC record includes completed review of major MVNO transactions. Transfer probability is limited by founder dependence, wholesale consent, carrier acquisition of successful resellers, regulatory approvals, customer churn during migration, and the fact that many small providers will close or lose a distribution agreement rather than complete a qualifying sale.

principal_driver: A facility-light model concentrates labor in repeat digital commercial, support, billing, and provisioning workflows suited to controlled AI assistance.
principal_weakness: Wholesale-carrier dependence, price competition, regulatory exposure, and the absence of a defensible LMM firm count constrain the acquisition thesis.

sources.supports: Defines resellers as purchasing access and network capacity and reselling wired or wireless service without operating transmission facilities, with MVNO, prepaid, wired, wireless, carrier, agent, and satellite boundaries.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 517121 Telecommunications Resellers

sources.supports: Reports the broad telecom occupation mix, including 29.67% installation and repair, 17.82% sales, 16.04% computer and mathematical, 14.85% office support, 8.05% business operations, and 7.33% management.
sources.title: BLS May 2023 Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 517000 Telecommunications

sources.supports: Describes product and service information, orders, cancellations, complaints, interaction records, billing, and issue resolution tasks relevant to reseller customer care.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Customer Service Representatives

sources.supports: Describes prospecting, needs discussions, proposals, account records, cost and term estimates, technical explanations, support, and negotiation.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Sales Representatives of Services

sources.supports: Describes increasing competitive relevance of cable MVNOs, many MVNO providers, wholesale purchase and resale, aggressive bundles, carrier concentration, and prior reviewed MVNO control transactions.
sources.title: FCC Memorandum Opinion and Order on T-Mobile and UScellular Applications

sources.supports: Defines resellers for universal-service reporting and documents revenue classification, recordkeeping, certification, enforcement, customer-base acquisitions, and recurring and nonrecurring telecommunications charges.
sources.title: FCC Form 499-Q Instructions, September 2024

sources.supports: Projects broad telecommunications constant-dollar output from $674.3 billion in 2024 to $876.8 billion in 2034, a 2.7% annual growth rate, while employment declines 0.9% annually.
sources.title: BLS Employment and Output by Industry, 2024-2034

title: Telecommunications Resellers
